question_text: Consumer protection means safeguarding the interests and rights of:
explanation: Consumer protection refers to safeguarding the interests and rights of consumers against unfair trade practices, defective goods, and deficient services. It empowers consumers to seek redressal.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Manufacturers against raw material shortage
Consumers against unfair trade practices and defective goods
Wholesalers against price fluctuations
Government against tax evasion

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer protection is about consumers, not manufacturers' supply chain issues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer protection with producer protection

elimination_hints.hint: Wholesalers are intermediaries. Consumer protection focuses on the end consumer.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer with wholesaler

elimination_hints.hint: Tax evasion is a government revenue concern, not consumer protection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer protection with taxation

question_text: Which of the following is a consumer right under the Consumer Protection Act?
explanation: The Right to Safety protects consumers against goods and services that are hazardous to life and property. It is one of the six consumer rights recognised under the Consumer Protection Act 2019.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Right to Safety
Right to Vote
Right to Strike
Right to Property

elimination_hints.hint: Right to Vote is a political right under the Constitution, not a consumer right.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer rights with political rights

elimination_hints.hint: Right to Strike is a labour right, not a consumer right.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer rights with labour rights

elimination_hints.hint: Right to Property is a constitutional/legal right, not a consumer right under CPA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer rights with constitutional rights

question_text: How many consumer rights are recognised under the Consumer Protection Act 2019?
explanation: The CPA 2019 recognises six consumer rights: Right to Safety, Right to be Informed, Right to Choose, Right to be Heard, Right to Seek Redressal, and Right to Consumer Education.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Three
Four
Six
Ten

elimination_hints.hint: Three is too few. The CPA 2019 provides six distinct consumer rights.
elimination_hints.misconception: Underestimating the number of consumer rights

elimination_hints.hint: Four is incorrect. There are six consumer rights — Safety, Information, Choose, Heard, Redressal, Education.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recalling an incomplete list

elimination_hints.hint: Ten is too many. The CPA 2019 specifically lists six consumer rights.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the number of consumer rights

question_text: The Right to be Informed means consumers have the right to:
explanation: The Right to be Informed ensures consumers receive complete information about the quality, quantity, potency, purity, standard, and price of goods or services so they can make informed decisions.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Demand free replacement of any product at any time
Know the quality, quantity, price, and other details of goods or services
Get unlimited credit from sellers
Inspect the factory where goods are produced

elimination_hints.hint: Free replacement relates to Right to Seek Redressal, not Right to be Informed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing right to information with right to redressal

elimination_hints.hint: Credit is a financial matter, not about being informed about product details.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer rights with credit rights

elimination_hints.hint: Factory inspection is not a consumer right. The right is to information about the product itself.
elimination_hints.misconception: Extending the right to information beyond its scope

question_text: The Right to Choose implies that a consumer:
explanation: The Right to Choose ensures consumers have access to a variety of goods and services at competitive prices. No seller can force a consumer to buy a particular product through tied selling or restrictive practices.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Must buy only branded products
Must accept whatever the seller offers
Can select from a variety of goods at competitive prices
Can take goods without paying

elimination_hints.hint: Right to Choose means freedom, not restriction to branded products only.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking right to choose restricts options

elimination_hints.hint: This is the opposite of Right to Choose. Consumers are NOT forced to accept any offer.
elimination_hints.misconception: Negating the right to choose

elimination_hints.hint: Taking goods without paying is theft. Right to Choose means freedom of selection, not free goods.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing right to choose with right to free goods

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a consumer responsibility?
explanation: Inspecting manufacturing units is not a consumer responsibility. Consumer responsibilities include being aware of rights, buying from authorised dealers, reading labels, keeping receipts, and filing complaints when wronged.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Being aware of consumer rights
Asking for a cash memo or receipt
Filing a complaint when exploited
Inspecting the manufacturing unit of the seller

elimination_hints.hint: Awareness of rights is the first consumer responsibility — you can't exercise rights you don't know.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking awareness is not a responsibility

elimination_hints.hint: Asking for receipts is important — it serves as proof of purchase for any complaint.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking receipts are not the consumer's responsibility

elimination_hints.hint: Filing complaints is both a right and a responsibility — consumers should not stay silent when exploited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking filing complaints is not a responsibility

question_text: The Consumer Protection Act 2019 replaced the earlier Act of:
explanation: The Consumer Protection Act 2019 replaced the Consumer Protection Act 1986. The new Act came into force on 20th July 2020 and introduced several changes including e-commerce coverage and the Central Consumer Protection Authority.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: 1970 is too early. The first Consumer Protection Act was enacted in 1986.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect year for original CPA

elimination_hints.hint: No CPA was enacted in 2000. The original was 1986, replaced by 2019.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect year

elimination_hints.hint: 2013 had the Companies Act, not CPA. The original CPA was 1986.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing with Companies Act

question_text: The three-tier consumer redressal machinery consists of:
explanation: The Consumer Protection Act establishes a three-tier redressal system: District Commission (district level), State Commission (state level), and National Commission (national level). Appeals go upward.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: District Commission, State Commission, National Commission
Panchayat, Municipality, Parliament
Police Station, High Court, Supreme Court
Village Court, District Court, High Court

elimination_hints.hint: These are political/governance bodies, not consumer redressal forums.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer forums with political bodies

elimination_hints.hint: These are criminal/civil courts. Consumer commissions are separate quasi-judicial bodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer forums with regular courts

elimination_hints.hint: These are regular judicial courts. The CPA establishes specialised consumer commissions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer forums with judiciary

question_text: Which of the following can file a complaint under the Consumer Protection Act?
explanation: A consumer, any recognised consumer association, the Central/State Government, or a group of consumers with the same interest can file a complaint. Legal heirs of a deceased consumer can also file.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Only the consumer who purchased the product
Only a lawyer on behalf of the consumer
Only the Central Government
Consumer, consumer association, Central/State Government, or group of consumers

elimination_hints.hint: 'Only' is too restrictive. Consumer associations and the government can also file complaints.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking only the buyer can file

elimination_hints.hint: A lawyer is not required. Consumers can file complaints directly without legal representation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking legal representation is mandatory

elimination_hints.hint: 'Only' the Central Government is wrong. Consumers themselves, associations, and state govts can also file.
elimination_hints.misconception: Restricting filing rights to government only

question_text: Consumer organisations play an important role in consumer protection by:
explanation: Consumer organisations (NGOs) educate consumers about their rights, help them file complaints, test products, publish reports, and represent consumer interests before government bodies.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Manufacturing goods on behalf of consumers
Setting prices for all products in the market
Educating consumers about their rights and helping file complaints
Arresting sellers who cheat consumers

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer organisations don't manufacture goods. They educate and advocate for consumers.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing NGO role with manufacturing

elimination_hints.hint: Price setting is a government/market function. Consumer organisations create awareness.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing NGO role with price regulation

elimination_hints.hint: Arrest is a police function. Consumer organisations help with education, complaints, and awareness.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing NGO role with law enforcement

question_text: The Right to Seek Redressal means a consumer can:
explanation: The Right to Seek Redressal allows consumers to seek remedy when they have been wronged. This includes compensation, replacement, refund, or removal of defects in goods/services.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Seek compensation, replacement, or refund for defective goods/services
Force any seller to give a discount
Return any product even if there is no defect
Demand free goods from the government

elimination_hints.hint: Redressal is for genuine grievances (defects, fraud), not for demanding arbitrary discounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Extending redressal to unreasonable demands

elimination_hints.hint: Redressal requires a genuine complaint — defect, deficiency, or unfair practice. Not for no-reason returns.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking redressal allows unconditional returns

elimination_hints.hint: Redressal is against the seller/service provider who wronged the consumer, not a demand on the government.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing redressal with government handouts

question_text: The Right to Consumer Education means:
explanation: The Right to Consumer Education ensures consumers have access to knowledge about their rights, remedies available, and how to protect themselves from exploitation. Government and NGOs play a role in spreading this awareness.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Consumers can attend any university for free
Sellers must train consumers to use products
Consumers must have a degree before buying goods
Consumers should have access to knowledge about their rights and remedies

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer education is about awareness of consumer rights, not free university admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer education with academic education

elimination_hints.hint: Product training may be after-sales service. Consumer education is about knowing your rights.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer education with product training

elimination_hints.hint: No educational qualification is required to be a consumer. The right is about awareness, not degrees.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding a non-existent precondition to buying

question_text: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, the District Commission can entertain complaints where the value of goods/services does not exceed:
explanation: Under CPA 2019, the District Commission has jurisdiction for complaints where the value of goods/services and compensation claimed does not exceed Rs 1 crore. Beyond this, the State Commission handles the case.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Rs 20 lakh
Rs 1 crore
Rs 10 crore
Rs 50 crore

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 20 lakh was the old limit under CPA 1986. CPA 2019 raised it to Rs 1 crore.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying old CPA 1986 limits

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 10 crore is the upper limit of the State Commission, not the District Commission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing District with State Commission limits

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 50 crore is beyond even the State Commission limit. It falls under the National Commission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing jurisdictions

question_text: The State Commission deals with complaints where the value of goods/services exceeds Rs 1 crore but does not exceed:
explanation: The State Commission handles cases where the value exceeds Rs 1 crore (District Commission's limit) but does not exceed Rs 10 crore. Above Rs 10 crore, the National Commission has jurisdiction.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Rs 1 crore
Rs 5 crore
Rs 10 crore
Rs 50 crore

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 1 crore is the District Commission's upper limit, not the State Commission's.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing District and State limits

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 5 crore is not a jurisdictional limit under CPA 2019. The State Commission goes up to Rs 10 crore.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect jurisdiction limit

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 50 crore is well within the National Commission's jurisdiction, not the State Commission's.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating State Commission jurisdiction

question_text: An appeal against the order of the District Commission can be filed before the:
explanation: The appeal hierarchy is: District Commission → State Commission → National Commission → Supreme Court. An appeal against District Commission must be filed before the State Commission within 45 days.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: District Court
State Commission
National Commission
Supreme Court

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer commissions are separate from regular courts. Appeals go to the next consumer commission level.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer forum with regular court hierarchy

elimination_hints.hint: National Commission hears appeals from the State Commission, not directly from the District Commission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping the State Commission level

elimination_hints.hint: Supreme Court is the final appeal court — only against National Commission orders, not District.
elimination_hints.misconception: Jumping to the highest court

question_text: Which of the following is an 'unfair trade practice'?
explanation: Making false claims about the quality, standard, or grade of goods is an unfair trade practice. Other examples include misleading advertising, false warranties, and hoarding to manipulate prices.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Making false claims about the quality or standard of goods
Offering genuine discounts during a sale
Providing accurate product information on labels
Giving a proper cash memo with every purchase

elimination_hints.hint: Genuine discounts are a legitimate business practice, not unfair.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all discounts are unfair

elimination_hints.hint: Accurate labelling is a fair and legal practice — it protects the consumer's Right to be Informed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking accurate information is unfair

elimination_hints.hint: Providing a cash memo is a responsible business practice, not an unfair one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking receipts are unfair practices

question_text: The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) established under CPA 2019 is empowered to:
explanation: The CCPA can regulate matters relating to violation of consumer rights, unfair trade practices, and misleading advertisements. It can impose penalties, order product recalls, and issue safety notices.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Only manufacture consumer goods
Only provide loans to consumers
Only conduct academic research on consumer behaviour
Regulate unfair trade practices, misleading ads, and protect consumer rights

elimination_hints.hint: CCPA is a regulatory body, not a manufacturing unit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing CCPA with a manufacturer

elimination_hints.hint: Loans are provided by banks/financial institutions. CCPA regulates consumer protection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing CCPA with a financial institution

elimination_hints.hint: Academic research is not CCPA's primary role. It actively regulates and penalises violations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Limiting CCPA to research only

question_text: Which of the following remedies is available to a consumer under the Consumer Protection Act?
explanation: The consumer commission can order: removal of defects, replacement of goods, refund of price, payment of compensation, discontinuation of unfair trade practice, or award of adequate costs.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Imprisonment of the seller only
Cancellation of the seller's citizenship
Removal of defects, replacement, refund, or compensation
Seizure of the seller's personal property

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer commissions primarily award compensation and corrective orders, not imprisonment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking consumer law is criminal law

elimination_hints.hint: Cancelling citizenship is not a legal remedy under any consumer law.
elimination_hints.misconception: Imagining extreme penalties

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer commissions order specific remedies (refund, replacement), not seizure of personal property.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer remedies with asset seizure

question_text: Consumer protection is important from the business point of view because:
explanation: Consumer protection benefits businesses by building long-term customer loyalty and trust. Satisfied, protected consumers become repeat buyers, generate positive word-of-mouth, and strengthen the brand's reputation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It allows businesses to increase prices without limit
It helps build long-term customer trust and loyalty
It eliminates all competition in the market
It allows businesses to avoid paying taxes

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer protection actually restrains unfair pricing, not enables unlimited price hikes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking consumer protection favours price increases

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer protection promotes fair competition, not elimination of competition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer protection with monopoly

elimination_hints.hint: Tax is a separate legal obligation. Consumer protection has nothing to do with tax avoidance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing consumer protection with tax matters

question_text: ISI mark and AGMARK are examples of:
explanation: ISI mark (for industrial products) and AGMARK (for agricultural products) are quality certification marks. They assure consumers that the product meets prescribed quality standards, supporting the Right to Safety and Right to be Informed.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Quality certification marks that assure product standards
Brand names of private companies
Types of consumer commissions
Categories of unfair trade practices

elimination_hints.hint: ISI and AGMARK are government-issued certification marks, not private brand names.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing government certifications with brand names

elimination_hints.hint: Consumer commissions are District, State, and National. ISI/AGMARK are product quality marks.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing quality marks with legal bodies

elimination_hints.hint: Unfair trade practices include fraud, misleading ads. ISI/AGMARK certify fair quality.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing quality assurance with unfair practice
